text: Laparoscopic surgery in Crohn's disease has been controversial since it was introduced more than ten years ago. There are technical challenges like friable bowel condition, thickened mesentery and risk of bleeding.
Questions have been raised if lesions may be missed and if there are any advantages at all.
Nowadays, even in complex Crohn's disease cases with previous operations, sealed perforations and enteric fistulas, laparoscopic surgery may be carried out.
Four cases will be presented in this video.
Case one, a male patient of seventy-four years with late Crohn's manifestation, had had previous open ileocecal resection and open cholecystectomy.
The surgeon examines the neoterminal ileum, identifying a recurrence with an abscess that had been previously drained.
The laparoscopic camera is introduced through a trocar placed below the umbilicus, providing a view of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and take down multiple adhesions of the omentum from the peritoneum.
The surgeon reaches the area of small bowel loops, visible within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon employs blunt dissection techniques to separate tissue layers, intermittently using the harmonic scalpel for precise cutting.
The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneum using laparoscopic scissors, separating the tissue layers under laparoscopic view.
The surgeon inspects the immediate area of dissection closely, ensuring the bowel surface remains uninjured during the procedure.

Following complete adhesiolysis, the surgeon visually inspects and manipulates the small bowel, running it from the ligament of Treitz down towards the ileum, ensuring no remaining adhesions are present.
The surgeon identifies additional adhesions on the small bowel and carefully removes them using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon reaches the neoterminal ileum, which exhibits a fat-creeping pattern indicative of recurrent disease.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the ileum and adjacent ascending colon, including the anastomosis, progressing towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon applies traction and countertraction to facilitate the dissection, ensuring careful manipulation of the bowel surface to prevent injury.
The surgeon employs both blunt and sharp dissection techniques to manipulate and separate tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a mini-laparotomy by partially excising the scar from the previous midline incision, extending approximately seven centimeters.
The surgeon extracts the mobilized bowel through the incision and examines it, revealing the neoterminal ileum with its characteristic fat-creeping pattern.
The surgeon demonstrates the diseased and non-diseased ileum, highlighting the differences in appearance between the affected and unaffected sections of the bowel.
The surgeon transects the colon, visibly separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon includes an inflammatory polyp in the resection, visually identifying and excising the polyp from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon transects the ileum a few centimeters proximal to the diseased area, using surgical instruments to separate the bowel segment.
The surgeon exposes the resected specimen, revealing the thickened mesentery and bowel wall, indicative of inflammation or disease.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis using interrupted sutures in a single layer, before returning the bowel to the peritoneal cavity.
Case two is a female patient of nineteen years. She has a three-year history of Crohn's disease.
The surgeon identifies an enteroenteric fistula formation between segments of the bowel, visible as an abnormal connection between the intestinal loops.
The surgeon inspects an inflammatory mass in the right lower quadrant, with a small bowel loop visibly adherent to the mass.
The camera view reveals the omentum attached to the inflammatory mass and adherent small bowel loop in the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon inspects the small bowel thoroughly for additional Crohn's lesions, examining the tissue for signs of inflammation or abnormality.
The surgeon examines the inflammatory tumor, revealing involvement of both the small and large bowel, with visible adhesions between these structures.
A loop of non-diseased ileum is visible hanging down towards the pelvis between two adherent bowel segments.
The surgeon explores the abdominal cavity, revealing the primarily diseased terminal ileum on the left side, which is fistulizing to a longer segment of secondarily involved ileum.
It is not separated immediately.
The surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the bowel, focusing on the clear margin of the inflamed region to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel with traction and counter traction to dissect the inflamed bowel segments, separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon proceeds with a stepwise approach to manage the large mass, carefully navigating around the adherent bowel segments and the non-diseased ileum loop hanging towards the pelvis.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery of the ileum towards its root, ensuring complete mobilization of the affected intestinal segment.
The surgeon uses blunt instruments to assist in the mobilization of the inflamed region, providing careful manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon visually inspects the right ureter to ensure it is not injured during the procedure.
The surgeon continues mobilizing the bowel cephalad towards the hepatic flexure, using traction and counter traction techniques.
The camera view shifts to focus on the gallbladder and liver, revealing their anatomical positioning and surface characteristics.
The surgeon exteriorizes the mobile mass through a peri-umbilical incision, then proceeds to take down and resect the attached loop of the ilium.
The surgeon performs an ileo-colectomy to resect the main lesion, followed by an end-to-end anastomosis to connect the resected bowel segments.
Case three is a thirty-year-old male patient with a history of Crohn's disease of fifteen years.
The video shows a sealed perforation in the terminal ileum, with visible tissue changes indicating the site of perforation.
The surgeon begins at the ligament of Treitz and runs the small bowel distally using a hand-over-hand technique with two bowel clamps, visually inspecting the bowel as it is manipulated.
The ileum is observed disappearing beneath an inflammatory tumor with a smooth surface.
Epiploic appendices of the neighboring sigmoid colon are visibly adherent to surrounding structures.
The surgeon removes the epiploic appendices that are obstructing the surgical field, detaching them from the neighboring sigmoid colon.
The surgeon begins lateral mobilization in the area of the ascending colon, moving proximally towards the ileum, using a different approach compared to previous cases.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to rapidly separate tissue, demonstrating efficient dissection technique.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to facilitate dissection and maintain a clear view of the anatomical structures during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the inflammatory mass with a sealed perforation located towards the iliac fossa, highlighting the remaining structure after previous dissection steps.
The video shows firm and dense adhesions caused by chronic inflammation over many years, visible as thickened tissue bands within the surgical field.
Experience is demanded to take down such an area of fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon completes mobilization along the upper part of the ascending colon and hepatic flexure, reaching the attachments of the omentum.
The surgeon performs a mini laparotomy above and below the umbilicus, creating small incisions in the abdominal wall.
The incision is made large enough to exteriorize the bulky bowel loops, allowing for the manipulation and examination of the bowel outside the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to transect the bowel on both sides, removes the specimen, and performs an end-to-end anastomosis, visually connecting the bowel ends.
Case four is a special case of a twenty-five-year-old female patient with a history of open appendectomy.
The video shows enteroenteric fistulas in the ileocecal region caused by Crohn's disease, with visible abnormal connections between bowel loops.
An internist had applied a capsule camera for endoluminal diagnostics.
The capsule camera is visibly lodged in front of a stenotic segment of the bowel, unable to progress further.
An x-ray image displays the capsule camera positioned within the intestinal tract.
During exploration, adhesions between the small bowel and omentum are visible.
This is the main area of disease.
Loops of the terminal ileum are visibly adhered to each other and extend towards the pelvis, indicating the presence of adhesions.
Altogether, however, they are quite mobile.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform sharp and blunt dissection, mobilizing the bowel from lateral to medial.
The surgeon transects the colon just above the cecum, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon palpates the ileum carefully to locate the last camera, manipulating the bowel to identify its position.
The surgeon incises the bowel wall longitudinally on the side opposite the mesentery, harvesting the capsule from the exposed area.
The final view shows the completed hand-sewn end-to-end ileocolostomy, with the ileum and colon visibly joined together.
There were no postoperative complications.
A short incision measuring 4.5 centimeters is made around the umbilicus, preserving the young lady's tattoo.
